segments.title: Why the Seven Years' War looks like a world war
segments.summary: The trailer introduces the Seven Years' War as an unusually broad case for thinking about conflict on a global scale.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke opens by asking why the Seven Years' War is so often used as a starting point for strategic theory. He presents it as a plausible early world war because it brings several kinds of conflict into one historical case. That breadth makes it useful for comparing armies, navies, colonies, and wider state policy rather than treating war as a single-front problem.

evidence.note: The introduction calls the war a possible first world war and says it contains every kind of strategic case.

segments.title: One war, contrasting land and maritime strategies
segments.summary: The Seven Years' War can support arguments about mass armies ashore as well as maritime strategy across a global theatre.
segments.body: The trailer contrasts a land-focused reading built around mass armies and major fighting ashore with a maritime reading that spans the globe. Neither view is treated as incidental: the same war supplies material for both. The comparison is a useful reminder that naval power has to be understood alongside the land forces, territories, and political aims it connects.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts mass armies fighting ashore with a globe-spanning maritime strategy.

segments.title: How strategic theorists use a familiar but distant war
segments.summary: A war can become attractive to theorists when it is familiar enough to invoke but distant enough to simplify into an example.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that many theorists approach the Seven Years' War with an argument already in mind and seek an historical illustration for it. Its name is widely recognised, but its details are less immediately contested than those of more recent wars. That combination can make it tempting to use the conflict as a clean strategic example while overlooking the complications that would challenge the lesson.

evidence.note: The transcript explains why theorists seek a known war whose context and nuance are less likely to be challenged.

segments.title: Why grand strategy cannot be purely maritime or continental
segments.summary: The trailer cautions that a state may lean maritime or continental, but context prevents either approach from becoming a complete answer.
segments.body: Broad strategic formulas become less convincing once the particular context of a country and war is examined. Dr. Clarke compares strategy to economic policy: effective choices normally combine different measures rather than pursuing one pure model. A country may give maritime power or continental commitments priority, but it still has to understand the other side and be prepared to devote resources to it.

evidence.note: The closing argument says strategic thinking runs into context and rejects wholly pure maritime or continental strategies.
